---
title: "Create a component"
description: "Write a Viash component that fits OpenPipeline conventions"
order: 40
---

A component is one step of a pipeline: a script plus a `config.vsh.yaml` that
describes its inputs, outputs, dependencies, and container. OpenPipeline builds
every component with [Viash](https://viash.io). The how-to for writing a config
lives in the [Viash guide](https://viash.io/guide/) and
[config reference](https://viash.io/reference/config/); this page covers the
conventions specific to OpenPipeline and walks through the anatomy of a real
component.

## OpenPipeline conventions

:::{.callout-tip}
## Check the reference first
Before starting a new component, browse the
[reference](../reference/index.qmd) to see whether one already exists that does
what you need, or that you can extend.
:::

- **One common data format.** Every component reads and writes the same
  [MuData](../concepts/mudata.qmd) object. Any component can therefore slot
  before or after any other.
- **Where it lives.** A component sits in `src/<namespace>/<component>/`, grouped
  into [namespaces](project-structure.qmd#sec-project-structure) by function such
  as `convert`, `filter`, or `transform`.
- **Author info is centralized.** Each contributor has a YAML file in
  `src/authors/`. When you contribute code, add yourself to the component with
  Viash's `__merge__` directive so author details stay consistent across
  components. The role is either `author` or `contributor`:

  ```yaml
  authors:
    - __merge__: /src/authors/your_name.yaml
      roles: [ author ]
  ```

- **Resource labels.** Declare CPU, memory, and GPU needs with
  [resource labels](../guides/tune-resources.qmd) on the `nextflow` runner.
- **Base containers.** Start from a reputable, actively maintained base image and
  install dependencies through a package manager. Use the current tag rather than
  pinning an old one. Common choices are
  [`python`](https://hub.docker.com/_/python) for Python,
  [`ubuntu`](https://hub.docker.com/_/ubuntu) for Linux and bash,
  [`rocker`](https://rocker-project.org/) for R, and
  [`nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch`](https://catalog.ngc.nvidia.com/orgs/nvidia/containers/pytorch)
  for GPU-accelerated PyTorch.

## Anatomy of a component

The quickest way to start is to **copy the existing component closest to what you
need** and adapt it. A component folder holds the config, the script, and a test:

```
src/transform/log1p/
â”śâ”€â”€ config.vsh.yaml   # metadata: arguments, resources, container
â”śâ”€â”€ script.py         # the logic
â””â”€â”€ test.py           # the test (see Testing)
```

### The config

`config.vsh.yaml` declares the interface and the container. Trimmed from the real
[`transform/log1p`](https://github.com/openpipelines-bio/openpipeline/blob/main/src/transform/log1p/config.vsh.yaml):

```yaml
name: log1p
namespace: "transform"
description: |
  Logarithmize the data matrix. Computes X = log(X + 1).
authors:
  - __merge__: /src/authors/your_name.yaml
    roles: [ author ]
arguments:
  - name: "--input"
    type: file
    direction: input
    required: true
  - name: "--modality"
    type: string
    default: "rna"
  - name: "--output"
    type: file
    direction: output
    required: true
resources:
  - type: python_script
    path: script.py
  - path: /src/utils/setup_logger.py       # shared helpers, merged in
engines:
  - type: docker
    image: python:3.12-slim
    setup:
      - type: python
        __merge__: [/src/base/requirements/scanpy.yaml, .]
runners:
  - type: executable
  - type: nextflow
    directives:
      label: [midmem, lowcpu, highdisk]     # resource labels
```

A few OpenPipeline habits show up here: `--input`/`--output` are `file`
arguments carrying the [MuData](../concepts/mudata.qmd) object, a `--modality`
argument selects which modality to act on, shared Python requirements and helper
scripts are pulled in with `__merge__` instead of copied, and the `nextflow`
runner carries [resource labels](../guides/tune-resources.qmd).

### The script

The script does the work. OpenPipeline scripts read the MuData object, operate on
the requested modality, and write it back out. Every script opens with a `##
VIASH START` / `## VIASH END` block so it can be run standalone while iterating
(see [Build and test locally](development-workflow.qmd)):

```python
import mudata as mu
import scanpy as sc
import sys

## VIASH START
par = {"input": "input.h5mu", "output": "output.h5mu", "modality": "rna"}
meta = {"resources_dir": "src/utils"}
## VIASH END

# shared helpers listed under resources are available at runtime
sys.path.append(meta["resources_dir"])
from setup_logger import setup_logger
logger = setup_logger()

data = mu.read_h5ad(par["input"], mod=par["modality"])
data.X = sc.pp.log1p(data.X, copy=True)
data.write_h5mu(par["output"])
```

Viash replaces the `VIASH START`/`END` block with the real arguments at run time
and exposes them as `par`; `meta` carries build metadata such as
`resources_dir`, where the merged helper scripts land.

## Build and test

There is no separate build step while developing a component: `viash run` and
`viash test` compile it on the fly. See
[Build and test locally](development-workflow.qmd) for the loop and
[Testing](testing.qmd) for writing the test.

:::{.callout-tip}
## Descriptions become the reference
The `description` fields on the component and each argument are what the
[reference documentation](../reference/index.qmd) is generated from. Writing them
clearly is the same task as documenting your component.
:::

## Publishing

When your component is ready, open a pull request and follow
[Open a pull request](pull-requests.qmd). Once merged, Viash Hub builds it into
the package and publishes it with the next release.

**Next:** [Create a pipeline â†’](creating-pipelines.qmd)
